text: The surgeon addresses the right inguinal hernia by creating a peritoneal flap approximately four to five centimeters from the superior border of the inguinal canal. Blunt dissection is used to separate the peritoneum from preperitoneal structures. The inferior epigastric vessels are identified and remain on the anterior abdominal wall. The direct inguinal hernia, located medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, is reduced, exposing Cooper's ligament. The peritoneal lining is dissected from the gonadal vessels and spermatic cord, and preperitoneal adipose tissue is reduced from the inguinal canal. Once the space is large enough, the surgeon prepares to address the left inguinal hernia.

The surgeon performs dissection using a sweeping motion, avoiding sharp dissection or electrocautery. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible as the direct hernia sac is reduced. The space of Retzius is further developed and connected to the right-sided dissection plane. The triangle of pain, lateral to the gonadal vessels, is carefully dissected while staying close to the peritoneal lining to prevent injury.

The surgeon introduces ProGrip mesh into the adequately developed left inguinal space. The self-fixating mesh is oriented medially and then unfolded inferiorly, without the need for anchoring sutures or tacking devices. A 2-0 Stratifix suture is used to close each peritoneal pocket.

The surgeon positions the inferior edge of the mesh flush with the lower border of the dissected peritoneum to ensure stability during closure. This process is repeated on the opposite side, with the mesh pieces often overlapping at the medial ends in a narrow pelvis. Once the mesh covers all potential hernia defects, the peritoneal pockets are closed in a running fashion using a two O Stratafix suture.

The surgeon closes any defects in the peritoneal lining using Vicryl sutures to prevent intestinal adhesions, as the mesh lacks a protective barrier. A two O Stratafix suture, which is absorbable and barbed, is used to hold tension and facilitate closure of the prepared needle pocket. The needles are removed at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon places the excised bilateral cord lipomas into a specimen bag and removes them through an eight millimeter port.
